narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your full-time commitment to studies. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated your risk of failure at 53.1%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4th, which falls below the typical student's 5.53 average and was the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting a bar failure. Your upper-middle family income also contributed somewhat to this concern, as it stood out in a way that aligned with higher-risk patterns in the data. On a positive note, your LSAT score of 30, though below the average of 36.7, actually helped pull the prediction toward success more than it hurt. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 5—close to the average of 5.71—and undergraduate GPA of 3.0, near the typical 3.21, provided some reassurance, as did your full-time student status, which matches the vast majority of successful applicants. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your mid-range early performance and solid background were competitive, but the dip in later school rankings tipped the scales against you. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted growth. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later academic deciles with focused coursework, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews or bar prep support tailored for diverse applicants like yourself. Keep pushing; your story is far from over, and with these steps, you can reapply stronger next cycle. We're rooting for you.
